advantages: Provides a rigorous reason to use MLPs: non-linear hidden layers can represent continuous targets on compact domains, not just linear functions.
Reassures practitioners that lack of expressivity (in principle) is not the bottleneck for smooth bounded problems — focus can shift to data, optimization, and generalization.
Clarifies the role of width: a single hidden layer suffices in theory, simplifying the 'do we need depth at all?' discussion for beginners.
Separates representation from learning — a useful mental model when debugging high training error vs high validation error.
disadvantages: Existence proofs may require impractically large width or precise weights — the theorem gives no tight parameter budget for real datasets.
Can mislead teams into equating 'can approximate' with 'will generalize' or 'will train easily' — leading to oversized nets without regularization.
Compact-domain assumption may not match raw unbounded features; preprocessing and scaling become part of the practical story UAT does not cover.
Depth-efficiency results cut both ways: choosing only shallow nets for simplicity may waste compute on functions a deeper net represents more compactly.
